coredata.dc:title: Smartphone-based label-free ratiometric fluorescence detection of sertraline and glutathione based on the use of orange-emission carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Meng, Yating
Guo, Qiaozhi
Jiao, Yuan
Lei, Peng
Shuang, Shaomin
Dong, Chuan
coredata.dc:description: The novel label-free ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform was constructed for fluorescence and smartphone dual-mode determination of sertraline (Ser) and glutathione (GSH) based on orange emission carbon dots (O-CDs). The O-CDs was prepared using neutral red and polyethyleneimine as raw materials via a facile one-pot hydrothermal treatment. As the concentration of Ser increases, a new emission peak appears at the carbon dot at 533 nm, and the intensity of the emission peak at 593 nm does not vary, which is accompanied by the evident photoluminescence of the solution from orange to green. Besides, the ratio fluorescence of O-CDs@Ser can be effectively restored by introducing GSH, and the photoluminescence of the N-CDs solution changes from green to orange. Inspired by the fact, a smartphone sensing platform was developed for online analysis of Ser and GSH content. Moreover, the O-CDs have been favorably exploited for label-free ratiometric determination of Ser and GSH in PC-12 cells owing to its lower toxicity and excellent biocompatibility. Interestingly, the O-CDs can be used to manufacture O-CDs/starch photoluminescence powders and O-CDs/PVA photoluminescence flexible films. These findings may broaden the path for the development of orange emission carbon-based nanomaterials and their applications for ratiometric sensing, smartphone sensing, cellular imaging, and photovoltaic materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescent sensing
Smartphone sensing
Orange emission carbon dots
Ser
GSH

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence sensing of Ser and GSH
Label-free ratiometric detection of Ser and GSH
Mechanism of sertraline and GSH assay
Smartphone imaging detection of sertraline and GSH
Fluorescent powder and fluorescent flexible film

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21874087 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM, (B) dimensional distribution diagram, (C) FTIR, (D) XPS survey scan, (E) C1s, and (F) N1s of O-CDs. FTIR, Fourier transform infrared; O-CD, orange-emission carbon dot; TEM, transmission electron microscope; XPS, X-ray photoelectron microscopy.
(A) UV–Vis absorption and photoluminescence spectrum, (B) Excitation-independent PL behavior of O-CDs. PL, photoluminescence; O-CD, orange-emission carbon dot; UV–vis, ultraviolet visible.

(A) The PL titration of O-CDs with introduction of Ser, (B) the relationship between I533/I593 ratios versus Ser concentration, (C) fluorescence response of O-CDs/Ser system in the presence of various concentration of GSH, (D) the relationship between I533/I593 ratios and GSH concentration. GSH, glutathione; O-CD, orange-emission carbon dot; Ser, sertraline.
Chromaticity color coordinates of O-CDs, O-CDs@Ser and O-CDs@Ser@GSH.

The smartphone images of the O-CDs under ultraviolet light after adding different concentrations of (A) Ser and (B) GSH. Calibration curves obtained by plotting (R + G + B)/3 against (C) Ser and (D) GSH concentration. GSH, glutathione; O-CD, orange-emission carbon dot; Ser, sertraline.
The LCSM of O-CDs, O-CDs@Ser, and O-CDs@Ser@GSH in PC-12 cells. Scale bar: 20 μm. GSH, glutathione; O-CD, orange-emission carbon dot; Ser, sertraline.
The photographs of O-CDs/starch solid fluorescent powders under day light and UV light (365 nm). O-CD, orange-emission carbon dot.
The photographs of O-CDs/PVA fluorescent flexible film with different content of O-CDs under day light and UV light (365 nm). O-CD, orange-emission carbon dot.
Illustration of the construction of the label-free ratiometric sensing platform for fluorescence and smartphone dual-mode detection of Ser and GSH. GSH, glutathione; Ser, sertraline.
Schematic diagram of the O-CDs for the determination of Ser and GSH through the smartphone imaging. GSH, glutathione; O-CD, orange-emission carbon dot; Ser, sertraline.

head.ce:title: Smartphone-based label-free ratiometric fluorescence detection of sertraline and glutathione based on the use of orange-emission carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Jinzhong 030600, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Jinzhong 030600, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The novel label-free ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform was constructed for fluorescence and smartphone dual-mode determination of sertraline (Ser) and glutathione (GSH) based on orange emission carbon dots (O-CDs). The O-CDs was prepared using neutral red and polyethyleneimine as raw materials via a facile one-pot hydrothermal treatment. As the concentration of Ser increases, a new emission peak appears at the carbon dot at 533 nm, and the intensity of the emission peak at 593 nm does not vary, which is accompanied by the evident photoluminescence of the solution from orange to green. Besides, the ratio fluorescence of O-CDs@Ser can be effectively restored by introducing GSH, and the photoluminescence of the N-CDs solution changes from green to orange. Inspired by the fact, a smartphone sensing platform was developed for online analysis of Ser and GSH content. Moreover, the O-CDs have been favorably exploited for label-free ratiometric determination of Ser and GSH in PC-12 cells owing to its lower toxicity and excellent biocompatibility. Interestingly, the O-CDs can be used to manufacture O-CDs/starch photoluminescence powders and O-CDs/PVA photoluminescence flexible films. These findings may broaden the path for the development of orange emission carbon-based nanomaterials and their applications for ratiometric sensing, smartphone sensing, cellular imaging, and photovoltaic materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: As a zero-dimensional nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have been widely employed in fluorescence sensing [1–3], bioimaging [4–6], drug delivery [7–9], and other fields due to their excellent photostability [10–12], lower toxicity [13–15], and superior biocompatibility [16–18]. However, the emission of most reported CDs is in the short-wavelength emission region (blue to green), which is not auspicious to further biological applications [19–22]. Therefore, it is indispensable to exploit preparation strategies for long-wavelength emission CDs (orange to red).

Currently, some long-wavelength emitting CDs have been prepared and applied in the fields of bioimaging, light-emitting diodes, and fluorescence sensing [18,23–25]. Besides, a method for the preparation of long-wavelength emitting CDs using neutral red as a precursor has been developed. Gao et al. [26] initially synthesized CDs with red emission using neutral red and citrate as precursors and used them for Pt2+, Au3+, and Pt2+ sensing in cells and zebrafish, which provided a new idea to prepare long-wavelength emission CDs. Subsequently, some neutral red-based long-wavelength emission CDs preparation methods were also developed and used for the detection of ions, small molecules, and amino acids [2,27–30]. In order to further improve the sensing efficiency, ratiometric fluorescence sensing strategies based on long-wavelength emission CDs with neutral red as the precursor were also developed. However, achieving simultaneous intracellular and smartphone ratiometric sensing based on single CDs remains a formidable challenge.

Over the past few decades, selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) have been widely used as antidepressants to treat depression and obsessive-compulsive disorder [31,32]. SSRIs have become an emerging pollutant (EOCs) in non-target species in aquatic environments due to increased human activities and the release of exogenous SSRIs [33,34]. Sertraline (Ser, Scheme 1 , (1S-cis)-4-(3,4-dichlorophenyl)-1,2,3,4-tetrahydro-N-methyl-1-naphthyridinamine hydrochloride)), one of the SSRIs with the fewest side-effects [35,36]. Ser (0.997 μg/L) has been detected in surface waters of Asia, Europe, and North America, which adversely affects the reproduction and growth of aquatic animals [37,38]. Therefore, the detection of Ser in water bodies and biological systems is of great significance. Currently, the detection treatments for Ser are spectrophotometry [39,40], chromatography [41–44], and electrochemical methods [45–48]. These mainstream methods have the detriments of complicated operation and high cost. Fluorescence has attracted widespread attention due to its low cost and simple operation. However, the fluorescent assay for Ser has not been manufactured.

Glutathione (Scheme 2
), as the most abundant non-protein thiol in cells, is involved in the maintenance of intracellular redox homeostasis, metabolism, and detoxification [49,50]. The level of intracellular glutathione (GSH) is closely related to human immunodeficiency virus (HIV), liver injury, Parkinson's disease, and other diseases. At present, GSH detection methods are mainly capillary electrophoresis [51], electrochemiluminescence [52], electrochemistry [53], high performance liquid chromatography [54], and colorimetry [55]. However, these methods have the disadvantages of complicated operation, long response time, and high cost [56,57]. Fluorescent probes have attracted extensive attention due to their convenience, low cost, and high sensitivity. Recently, ratiometric fluorescence assay have been developed to monitor detector on the basis of changes in the dual-emission fluorescence intensity ratio, which can validly abstain the obstruction of background noise, instrument operation, local environmental changes, and sensor content changes. Several ratiometric fluorescent probes composed of CDs and metal organic framework [58], manganese dioxide [59], copper nanoclusters [60] have been developed and utilized for detection of GSH in real samples and living cells. Unfortunately, most of these ratiometric fluorescent probes are in the short-wavelength region, and the biologically spontaneous blue fluorescence is not auspicious to subsequent applications. It is worth noting that studies have shown that adding Ser can significantly reduce the level of intracellular GSH [61,62]. Therefore, it is a challenge to construct ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform based on CDs with long-wavelength emission to monitor Ser and GSH.

Inspired by the above-mentioned circumstances, a novel label-free ratiometric sensing platform based on orange-emission carbon dots (O-CDs) was designed and used for fluorescence and smartphones dual-mode detection of sertraline (Ser) and GSH. The O-CDs with excitation-independence wavelength were synthesized by a facilely hydrothermal treatment using neutral red (NR) and polyethyleneimine (PEI) as raw materials. After adding Ser, the emission spectra of O-CDs showed a novel peak at 533 nm, accompanied by the solution color from orange to green. When GSH was introduced into the O-CDs@Ser system, the emission peak at 533 nm gradually decreased, and the color of the solution returned to orange. Besides, a smartphone sensing platform based on O-CDs has also been fabricated for Ser and GSH detection. The O-CDs can be used for monitoring Ser and GSH in PC-12 cells owing to its fine biocompatibility and lower toxicity. Also, O-CDs can be utilized as phosphors and fluorescent flexible films due to their excellent optical properties. Overall, this study shows that the O-CDs have potential applications in ratiometric detection, smartphone sensing, and photovoltaic materials (Scheme 3).

Typically, the mentioned O-CDs were prepared by a hydrothermal method similar to the previous researches [2,28]. NR (5.9 mg) and PEI (M.W.1800, 10 mg) were synchronously placed in 20 mL DDI water. Then, the mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. Subsequently, dried O-CDs were obtained after filtration, dialysis, and lyophilization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence sensing of Ser and GSH
ce:section.ce:para: 200 μL of O-CDs solution (0.52 mg/mL) were added into 1.8 mL PBS (pH 7.4). To assess the application of Ser detection, 10 μL of different contents of Ser were mixed to O-CDs solutions. For GSH analysis, different contents of GSH was added into stock solution of O-CDs@Ser (110 μM). After reacting for 10 s under ambient condition, photoluminescence (PL) spectra were documented.

The mixed solution (O-CDs@Ser@GSH) was put into a black box and lighted with an ultraviolet lamp. The fluorescent images were gathered by camera of smartphone (Huawei P40pro). The gained images were trimmed, and R, G, B values were read by the smartphone App (Color Identification). Finally, the relationship between (R + G + B)/3 and Ser (or GSH) contents was fitted.

PC-12 cells were incubated in dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM) provided with 10% FBS. After hatch at 310 K for 0.5 h, the cells were rinsed three times with fresh pH = 7.4 phosphate buffered saline (PBS) to eliminate plethoric medium. In order to block the production of GSH in cells, PC-12 cells were incubated with N-ethylmaleimide 1.0 mM) for 0.5 h. After the cells were washed three times, they were incubated with PBS solution of O-CDs (30 min), and further incubated with Ser (60 μM). Subsequently, GSH was added to the sample (150 μM). PL images were acquired on a PL microscope using a 60 × oil-immersion objective.

In this work, the O-CDs were synthesized via one-pot hydrothermal strategy of NR and PEI at 180 °C for 6 h. The micromorphology of O-CDs was quested using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The TEM revealed that the O-CDs were uniformly dispersed with an average size of 2.93 nm (Fig. 1 B). High-resolution TEM images indicated high crystallinity with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which was attributed to the graphene pattern with C (100) lattice fringes (Fig. S1).

Then, the chemical structures of O-CDs were analyzed using Fourier transform infrared and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy spectrum. The vibrational absorption bands of the O-CDs (Fig. 1C) located at 3419 cm−1 (NH/OH stretching vibrations), 2946 and 2839 cm−1 (C–H stretching vibration), 1620 cm−1 (C=O stretching vibration), 1478 cm−1 (C–H stretching vibration), and 1117 cm−1 (C–O stretching vibration). Fig. 1D was the full X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy survey spectra of O-CDs, and there were three peaks at 280.6, 394.1, and 530.2 eV, indicating that O-CDs consisted of C, O, and N elements. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 1E) showed double unit moieties: C–C/C=C (284.5 eV) and C–N/C–O (285.0 eV). There are three peaks in the N1s band (Fig. 1F) at 399.1, 400.6, and 401.5 eV, representing that O-CDs contained pyridinic N, amino N, and pyrrolic N, respectively. The O1s peak (Fig. S2) exhibited two bands at 532.4 and 530.9 eV, which corresponded to C–O and C=O groups. These results are consistent with Fourier transform infrared analysis.

The unique optical properties of O-CDs were determined using UV–vis and fluorescence spectra. Three main features were measured in the UV–vis absorption spectrum, the absorption peak at 276 nm is mainly attributed to the n-π transition of C=C bond (Fig. 2 A). The broad absorption peaks centered at 366 nm and 456 nm may originate from the trapping surface state, which could be adopted to generate fluorescence [63,64]. The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths are 480 and 593 nm. According to 3D excitation-emission spectra (Fig.2B), O-CDs have excitation-independent wavelength. Normalized fluorescence spectra also confirmed this result (Fig. S3). Using Rhodamine B as a reference, quantum yield was calculated to be 12.9% (Fig. S4). In order to inspect the photostability of O-CDs, the effects of pH, NaCl, and irradiation time of xenon lamp on PL intensity of O-CDs were investigated. As shown in Fig. S5, the fluorescence intensity of O-CDs did not fluctuate significantly at pH 2–12. Although the concentration of KCl was as high as 2 mol/L, the photoluminescence of O-CDs remained unaffected (Fig. S6). The fluorescence intensity of O-CDs did not change after 3600 s continuous irradiation of the xenon lamp (Fig. S7). All the above results demonstrated the excellent photostability of O-CDs under physiological conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Label-free ratiometric detection of Ser and GSH
ce:section.ce:para: The latent sensing applications of O-CDs were investigated due to the distinct properties of the O-CDs surface. After adding Ser, a new peak appeared in the emission spectrum of O-CDs at 533 nm (Fig. 3 A). The ratio of I533/I593 showed a regular increase with the increase of Ser concentration. As shown in Fig. 3B, the intensity ratio at 533 nm and 593 nm was fitted using the Boltzmann equation, and the fitting equation was y = 1.811–2.526/1 + exp (x-18.61)/33.95 (R2 = 0.9980). In addition, I533/I593 exhibited a good linear relationship at 5–45 μM (inset of Fig. 3B, y = 0.1830 + 0.0192x, R2 = 0.9888), The limit od detection (LOD) was 0.96 μM. To further investigate the selectivity, the influences of metal ions, anion, and antidepressants on the photoluminescence of the O-CDs were inspected. As shown in Fig. S8, only Ser can increase the ratio of I533/I593, which proves that O-CDs can specifically recognize Ser. Compared with other Ser detection methods, the as-mentioned O-CDs have the advantages of high sensitivity and good selectivity (Table S1). To the best of our knowledge, this is the first Ser sensing method established based on CDs.

After GSH was introduced into the O-CDs@Ser system, its fluorescence intensity at 533 nm gradually decreased to almost the same fluorescence intensity as before (Fig. 3C). The intensity ratio at 533 nm and 593 nm was fitted using Boltzmann equation (Fig. 3D), and the fitting equation was y = −0.1957 + 1.820/((1 + exp (x-1.863)/0.4817)) (R2 = 0.9928). In addition, I533/I593 exhibited a good linear relationship at 10–110 μM (inset of Fig. 3D, y = 1.648-0.3030x, R2 = 0.9912), LOD was 2.62 μM. Moreover, to confirm the selectivity of GSH assay, other amino acids were inspected as control samples. Fig. S9 illustrated that the other amino acids had insignificant influence on the GSH detection, which proved that the O-CDs@Ser system had the vast potential for GSH determination. Compared with other GSH detection methods, the mentioned O-CDs have the advantages of low cost and short response time (Table S2). The fluorescence emissions of O-CDs, O-CDs@Ser, and O-CDs@Ser@GSH are further verified with CIE coordinates (0.5328, 0.4640), (0.4239, 0.5626), and (0.5352, 0.4615), which demonstrated the tremendous potential of O-CDs as a ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform in biosystem (Fig. 4 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of sertraline and GSH assay
ce:section.ce:para: To survey the possible interaction mechanism of O-CDs with Ser and GSH, UV–Vis absorption spectra, photoluminescence lifetime, dynamic light scattering, and zeta potential were measured. The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of Ser were shown in Fig. S10. The absorption peak of O-CDs at 367 nm was significantly enhanced after adding Ser (Fig. S11). After introducing GSH into the O-CDs@Ser system, the absorption peak at 367 nm recovered. After adding Ser, the PL lifetime of O-CDs did not change obviously (Fig. S12). The hydrodynamic diameters of O-CDs, O-CDs@Ser, and O-CDs@Ser@GSH are 78.82, 220.194, and 91.28 nm, respectively (Fig. S13). When Ser was added to the O-CDs solution, the thickness of the electric double layer of the O-CDs was damaged, which resulted in a smaller hydrodynamic diameter of the O-CDs@Ser. When GSH was put into the O-CDs@Ser mixture, Ser was eliminated from the surface of O-CDs and the hydrodynamic diameter was restored to 91.28 nm. The zeta potential also furnished the equally consequence (Fig. S14). All these results suggested that a stable complex may be formed between O-CDs and Ser, which further leads to a raising emission peak at 533 nm with unchanged intensity at 593 nm. After adding GSH, Ser could be competitively captured by GSH, and the photoluminescence of O-CDs was recovered.

To further illustrate the sensing performance of the fabricated O-CDs in complex environments, we used O-CDs as probes to detect Ser in human serum, human urine, sertraline tablets, and lake water. Table S3 shows that human serum, human urine, and lake water samples are free of Ser, while the Ser content in the four sertraline tablets is 14.6 μM. The recoveries for human serum, human urine, sertraline tablets, and lake water samples ranged from 86.9% to 107.2%, with RSDs all below 4.2% (n = 6).

The ability of O-CDs@Ser complex to specifically recognize GSH in complex system was explored by detecting the GSH content in asparagus, spinach, and human serum. Table S4 showed that the GSH levels in asparagus, spinach, and human serum were 9.2, 3.7, and 1.2 μM, respectively. The recoveries for asparagus, spinach, and human serum samples ranged from 96.2% to 103.5%, with RSDs all below 2.6% (n = 6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone imaging detection of sertraline and GSH
ce:section.ce:para: The color of O-CDs, O-CDs@Ser, and O-CDs@Ser@GSH solution changed from ‘orange-green-orange’ under UV light, so a detection strategy of Ser and GSH based on smartphone imaging was established (Fig. 5 A and B). Compared with naked eye visual detection, smartphone imaging detection method has the advantage of being more accurate. Scheme 4 illustrated the process of the established smartphone-based detection method for the determination of Ser and GSH. First, the camera of smartphone captured a series of fluorescent images. Second, colors were identified by smartphone software (color recognition) and converted to corresponding R, G, and B values (Tables S5 and S6). Finally, the R, G, and B values were entered in the smartphone software (WPS Office) to obtain the relationship between (R + G + B)/3 and Ser (or GSH) concentration. To further verify the accuracy of the proposed smartphone detection method, we choose (R + G + B)/3 as the abscissa, and the concentration of Ser (or GSH) as the ordinate to establish a linear relationship. The average value of R, G, and B can minimize the disturbance of the external environment during the image capture process. After adding Ser, the color of the solution changed from orange to green, and (R + G + B)/3 had a good linear relationship with Ser concentration (Fig. 5A and C, LOD = 2.76 μM). Continuing to add GSH, the color of the solution changed from green to orange, and (R + G + B)/3 had a good linear relationship with GSH concentration (Fig. 5B and D, LOD = 7.36 μM). All the above results demonstrated that the proposed smartphone detection method was capable of sensitively monitoring Ser and GSH.

The potential imaging application of O-CDs was further explored owing to their excellent optical properties. The cytotoxicity of O-CDs was investigated by 3-（4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyl-2-H-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay using PC-12 cells as a model. The O-CDs did not show significant cytotoxicity in the range of 0–500 μg/mL, which indicating that O-CDs have favorable biocompatibility and are appropriate for live cell imaging (Fig. S15).

The probability of O-CDs for cell imaging and monitoring of Ser and GSH was probed by introducing them into PC-12 cells. Images of the first and second columns were recorded in green channel (510–550 nm) and orange channel (560–620 nm) by 488 nm laser, respectively (Fig. 6 ). PC-12 cells initially displayed orange fluorescence in channel 2 and no significant fluorescence in channel 1. After adding Ser, the fluorescence of channel 2 did not change significantly, but channel 1 showed strong green fluorescence. Continuing to add GSH, the fluorescence of both channels returned to the original fluorescence. The fluorescence ratio images and the dual-channel overlay images were obtained by Zeiss ZEN software and showed distinct and regularly changing colors in PC-12 cells, which confirmed the ability of O-CDs to specifically recognize Ser and GSH in biological systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent powder and fluorescent flexible film

ce:section.ce:para: In conclusion, we designed a ratiometric sensing platform based on O-CDs for dual-mode determination of Ser and GSH by fluorescence and smartphone. The O-CDs were facilely synthesized by a one-pot hydrothermal strategy using NR and PEI as precursors. The as-prepared O-CDs can be used for ratiometric determination of sertraline and glutathione on the basis of ratiometric fluorescence changes at 533 and 593 nm and reversible orange-to-green luminescence changes. Meanwhile, smartphone makes color recognition more accurate and successfully realize the visual detection of Ser and GSH. The O-CDs successfully used for the ratiometric detection of intracellular Ser and GSH and the preparation of fluorescent powders and fluorescent flexible films owing to their excellent optical properties and good biocompatibility. The O-CDs are promising long-wavelength carbon-based nanomaterials for ratiometric detection, smartphone sensing, and photovoltaic materials.
Yating Meng: Analyzed the data, Resources, Writing-original draft, Writing-review & editing.
Qiaozhi Guo: Investigation, Discussed the results.
Yuan Jiao: Formal analysis, Discussed the results.
Peng Lei: Supervision.
Shaomin Shuang: Project administration, Supervision.
Chuan Dong: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21874087).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtchem.2022.101170.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis of orange fluorescence multifunctional carbon dots for label-free detection of vitamin B12 and endogenous/exogenous peroxynitrite
J. Hazard Mater.
